---
title: The Last Byte
subtitle: '**From Raw Data to Well-Done Visualizations**'
author:
  - name: 'Kevin Madden <br> *Assistant Director of Annual Giving* <br> *Villanova University*'
    # email: <br> *kevin.madden@villanova.edu*
date: 15 July 2026
date-format: long
format:
    revealjs:
        embed-resources: true
        self-contained-math: true
        smaller: true
        scrollable: true
#        reference-location: document
        theme: dracula_custom.scss
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r initial-libraries}
#| echo: false
#| include: false

library(pacman)
p_load(tidyverse)
```

## Before We Begin...
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
*What is your level of experience with data visualizations?*
:::

```{r quiz1-qr}
#| echo: false
#| include: false

p_load(qrcode)

quiz1 <- qr_code('https://strawpoll.com/w4nWWpQXlnA')

png(filename = 'quiz1.png', bg = 'transparent')
par(mar = c(0, 0, 0, 0), oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0), xaxs = 'i', yaxs = 'i')
plot(quiz1, col = c('#282a36','#f8f8f2'), main = NA)
dev.off()
```

![](./quiz1.png){fig-align="center" height=20%}

## Pulse Check Results

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=20%}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![*In case you're just now joining us, vote here!*](./quiz1.png){fig-align="center" height=20%}
:::
:::

::: {.column width=80%}
<div class="strawpoll-embed" id="strawpoll_w4nWWpQXlnA" style="height: 600px; max-width: 640px; height: 100%; width: 100%; margin: 0 auto; display: flex; flex-direction: column;"><iframe title="StrawPoll Embed" id="strawpoll_iframe_w4nWWpQXlnA" src="https://strawpoll.com/embed/w4nWWpQXlnA/results" style="position: static; visibility: visible; display: block; width: 100%; flex-grow: 1;" scrolling = "no" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen allowtransparency>Loading...</iframe><script nonce="MjVlOTZlZjc4YTdkMDcxNGUwYjA2ZDBmODI3ZWJjMWNiZTljNGM5NQ==" async src="https://cdn.strawpoll.com/dist/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script></div>
:::
::::

## Session Overview

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width='45%'}
<u>What we *WON'T* focus on</u>

::: {.fragment}
- Data sourcing/wrangling/mining
  - [Extract-Transform-Load (ETL)](https://www.databricks.com/discover/etl)
  - [Cross Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)](https://www.datascience-pm.com/crisp-dm-2/)
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Programming or software tutorials
  - [R](https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/) and [Python](https://nostarch.com/python-crash-course-3rd-edition)
  - [Power Query M formula language](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powerquery-m/)
  - [Data Analysis Expressions (DAX)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/)
:::
:::

::: {.column width='10%'}
:::

::: {.column width='45%'}
<u>What we *WILL* focus on</u>

::: {.fragment}
- Data viz theory
  - Perceptual rankings
  - Preattentive attributes
  - Gestalt principles of visual perception
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Data viz best practices
  - What makes visualizations good (and not-so-good)
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Types of visualizations and their use cases
  - Advancement-specific examples
:::
:::

::::

# Data Viz Theory

## So...Why Visualizations?

Why not just stick to Excel sheets or tables with raw numbers?

. . .

Let's start with an essential example:

### Anscombe's Quartet

::: {.fragment}
:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=70%}
- Published in 1973 by English statistician Francis Anscombe.
- The Quartet is comprised of 4 sets of paired x- and y-values.
  - *x*: horizontal axis
  - *y*: vertical axis
- Let's take a look at the data and see what we observe!
:::

::: {.column width=30%}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![Portrait of Francis J. Anscombe (Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank_Anscombe))](./Francis_Anscombe.jpeg){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::
::::
:::

## Anscombe's Quartet
```{r anscombe}
p_load(gt, gtExtras)

anscombe_data <- anscombe %>% 
  mutate(obs = row_number()) %>% 
  select(9,1,5,2,6,3,7,4,8)

anscombe_table <- gt(anscombe_data) %>% 
  tab_header(
    title = 'Observations'
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'Set 1', columns = ends_with('1')
  ) %>% 
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'Set 2', columns = ends_with('2')
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'Set 3', columns = ends_with('3')
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'Set 4', columns = ends_with('4')
  ) %>% 
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(2:9),
    decimals = 2
  ) %>% 
  cols_label(
    obs = 'Obs. No.',
    x1 = 'x',
    y1 = 'y',
    x2 = 'x',
    y2 = 'y',
    x3 = 'x',
    y3 = 'y',
    x4 = 'x',
    y4 = 'y'
  ) %>% 
  cols_align(
    align = 'center'
  ) %>% 
  tab_options(
    table.font.color.light = '#f8f8f2',
    table.background.color = '#282a36',
    column_labels.font.weight = 'bold'
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = 'bold'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'obs')
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = 'bold'),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything())
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#e2cffc'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = ends_with('1')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = ends_with('1')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = ends_with('1'))
    )
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#f1fa8c'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = ends_with('2')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = ends_with('2')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = ends_with('2'))
    )
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#ff79c6'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = ends_with('3')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = ends_with('3')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = ends_with('3'))
    )
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#50fa7b'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = ends_with('4')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = ends_with('4')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = ends_with('4'))
    )
  )
  
# anscombe_table

anscombe_tidy <- anscombe %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = c('.value', 'Set'),
    names_pattern = '(.)(.)')

anscombe_summary <- anscombe_tidy %>% 
  group_by(Set) %>% 
  summarize(
    x_mean = mean(x),
    x_var = var(x),
    x_sd = sd(x),
    y_mean = mean(y),
    y_var = var(y),
    y_sd = sd(y),
    corr = cor(x, y),
    r_sq = summary(lm(y~x))$r.squared
  )

anscombe_sumtable <- gt(anscombe_summary) %>% 
  tab_header(
    title = 'Summary Statistics'
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'x', columns = starts_with('x')
  ) %>% 
  tab_spanner(
    label = 'y', columns = starts_with('y')
  ) %>% 
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(2:9),
    decimals = 2
  ) %>% 
  cols_label(
    x_mean = 'Mean',
    x_var = 'Variance',
    x_sd = 'SD',
    y_mean = 'Mean',
    y_var = 'Variance',
    y_sd = 'SD',
    corr = 'Correlation',
    r_sq = 'R\u00b2'
  ) %>% 
  cols_align(
    align = 'center'
  ) %>% 
  tab_options(
    table.font.color.light = '#f8f8f2',
    table.background.color = '#282a36',
    column_labels.font.weight = 'bold',
    table.margin.left = px(50)
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = 'bold'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'Set')
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = 'bold'),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything())
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#e2cffc'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'Set', rows = Set == 1)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#f1fa8c'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'Set', rows = Set == 2)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#ff79c6'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'Set', rows = Set == 3)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#50fa7b'),
    locations = cells_body(columns = 'Set', rows = Set == 4)
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#ffb86c'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = starts_with('x')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = starts_with('x')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = starts_with('x'))
    )
  ) %>% 
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = '#8be9fd'),
    locations = list(
      cells_body(columns = starts_with('y')),
      cells_column_spanners(spanners = starts_with('y')),
      cells_column_labels(columns = starts_with('y'))
    )
  )
  
# anscombe_sumtable

anscombe_grp <- gt_two_column_layout(list(anscombe_table, anscombe_sumtable))
```

::: {.r-stack}
```{r anscombe-tables}
anscombe_grp
```
:::

## Anscombe's Quartet
```{r anscombe-ggplot}
anscombe_plt <- ggplot(anscombe_tidy, aes(x = x, y = y, color = Set)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c('#e2cffc', '#f1fa8c', '#ff79c6', '#50fa7b')) +
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = FALSE, color = '#d33682', formula = y ~ x) +
  facet_wrap(~ Set, labeller = labeller(.default = ~ paste('Set',.x))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    strip.text = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', size = 12),
    axis.text = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2'),
    axis.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', face = 'italic'),
    panel.grid = element_line(color = '#6272a4'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )
```

::: {.r-stack}
```{r anscombe-plot}
anscombe_plt
```
:::

## Perceptual Hierarchy

::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![Source: [The Truthful Art](https://learning.oreilly.com/library/view/the-truthful-art/9780133440492/) by Alberto Cairo [-@cairo-2016]](./perceptual_rankings.jpg){.lightbox fig-align="center" width=30%}
:::

## Preattentive Processing

::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![Some common preattentive attributes (Source: [Tableau Blueprint](https://help.tableau.com/current/blueprint/en-us/bp_why_visual_analytics.htm))](./preattentive_attribs.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::

## Gestalt Principles of Visual Perception

::: {style='text-align: center;'}
[<u>Gestalt</u> $\approx$ unified whole, configuration]{.highlight-orange}
:::

. . .

Our brains tend to group objects based on some of the following criteria:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width='47%'}
::: {.fragment}
- **Proximity**
  - The objects are close to one another.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Similarity**
  - The objects share features, such as color, size, shape, or direction.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Enclosure**
  - There is a defined boundary surrounding the objects.
:::
:::

::: {.column width='6%'}
:::

::: {.column width='47%'}
::: {.fragment}
- **Closure**
  - Gaps between the objects are suggestive of a whole.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Continuity**
  - The objects align or continue in a discernible order.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Connection**
  - The objects are connected to each other.
:::
:::
::::

## Examples of Gestalt Principles

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Proximity
```{r proximity}
set.seed(1217)

clust_A <- data.frame(
  x = rnorm(200, mean = 2, sd = 0.3),
  y = rnorm(200, mean = 2, sd = 0.3),
  group = 'A')

clust_B <- data.frame(
  x = rnorm(500, mean = 4, sd = 0.5),
  y = rnorm(500, mean = 4, sd = 0.5),
  group = 'B')

data <- rbind(clust_A, clust_B)

ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = '#ff79c6', alpha = 0.5, size = 3) +
  labs(title = 'Objects close to one another tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold')
  )
```

### Similarity
```{r similarity}
ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 3) +
  labs(title = 'Objects sharing similar attributes like color tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  scale_color_manual(values = c('#ffb86c', '#8be9fd')) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )
```

### Enclosure
```{r enclosure}
p_load(ggforce)
ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 3) +
  labs(title = 'Objects enclosed by a boundary tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  geom_mark_ellipse(aes(color = group)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c('#ffb86c', '#8be9fd')) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )
```

### Closure
```{r closure}
set.seed(1217)
close_data_missing <- data.frame(x = c(1:30)) %>% 
  mutate(
    y = ifelse(
      x %in% c(20:25),
      NA,
      rnorm(100, mean = 2, sd = 1)
    )
  )

close_data_expected <- close_data_missing %>% 
  filter(
    x == 19 | x == 26
  )

close_data_actual <- close_data_missing %>% 
  mutate(
    y = ifelse(
      x == 19 | x == 26,
      close_data_missing$y,
      ifelse(
        x < 20 | x > 25,
        NA,
        rnorm(100, mean = 4, sd = 1)
      )
    )
  )

p1 <- ggplot(close_data_missing, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = '#ffb86c') +
  labs(title = 'Our brains tend to fill in the gaps between objects, completing suggested patterns.') +
  ylim(-1,8) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold')
  )

p2 <- p1 +
  geom_line(data = close_data_expected, linewidth = 1, linetype = 'dashed', color = '#8be9fd')

p3 <- p2 +
  geom_line(data = close_data_actual, linewidth = 1, color = '#ffb86c', alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(subtitle = 'But sometimes, our brains are wrong!') +
  theme(
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'italic', size = 12)
  )
```

::: {.r-stack}
```{r closure-plot-1}
p1
```

::: {.fragment}
```{r closure-plot-2}
p2
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r closure-plot-3}
p3
```
:::
:::

### Continuity
```{r continuity}
cont_data <- data.frame(
  x = c('A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F', 'G'),
  y = c(100, 150, 90, 180, 200, 70, 120)
)

p1 <- ggplot(cont_data, aes(x = x, y = y))+
  geom_bar(stat = 'identity', fill = '#ffb86c') +
  labs(title = 'Our eyes more readily perceive smooth and continuous paths, lines, and curves as connected forms.') +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold')
  )

p2 <- ggplot(cont_data, aes(x = reorder(x, -y), y = y))+
  geom_bar(stat = 'identity', fill = '#8be9fd') +
  labs(title = 'Our eyes more readily perceive smooth and continuous paths, lines, and curves as connected forms.') +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold')
  )

# (p1 | p2) +
#   plot_annotation(
#     title = 'Our eyes more readily perceive smooth and continuous paths, lines, and curves as connected forms.',
#     theme = theme(
#       plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
#       panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
#       plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'))
#   )
```

::: {.r-stack}
```{r continuity-plot-1}
p1
```

::: {.fragment}
```{r continuity-plot-2}
p2
```
:::
:::

### Connection
```{r connection}
set.seed(1217)
connect_data <- data.frame(
  x = rep(1:50, 2),
  group = rep(c('A', 'B'), each = 50)) %>% 
    mutate(
      y = ifelse(
        group == 'A',
        rnorm(100, mean = 4, sd = 1),
        rnorm(100, mean = 2, sd = 1))
    )

p1 <- ggplot(connect_data, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = '#ff79c6') +
  labs(title = 'Connected objects tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  ylim(-2,10) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )

p2 <- ggplot(connect_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = 'Connected objects tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  scale_color_manual(values = c('#ffb86c', '#8be9fd')) +
  ylim(-2,10) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )

p3 <- ggplot(connect_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = 'Connected objects tend to be perceived as a group.') +
  scale_color_manual(values = c('#ffb86c', '#8be9fd')) +
  ylim(-2,10) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'),
    legend.position = 'none'
  )

# (p1 / p2 / p3) +
#   plot_annotation(
#     title = 'Connected objects tend to be perceived as a group.',
#     theme = theme(
#       plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
#       panel.background = element_rect(fill = '#282a36', color = NA),
#       plot.title = element_text(color = '#f8f8f2', hjust = 0.5, face = 'bold'))
#   )
```

::: {.r-stack}
```{r connection-plot-1}
p1
```

::: {.fragment}
```{r connection-plot-2}
p2
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r connection-plot-3}
p3
```
:::
:::

:::


## Data Viz Best Practices

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=30%}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![Can be accessed on [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.7312/schw19310) via your institution or purchased from [Columbia University Press](https://cup.columbia.edu/book/better-data-visualizations/9780231193115/)](https://cup-us.imgix.net/covers/9780231193115.jpg?auto=format&w=350){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

::: {.column width=70%}
In *Better Data Visualizations* [-@schwabish-2021], Jonathan Schwabish offers 5 guidelines for effective data visualizations:

::: {.incremental}
1. Show the data.
2. Reduce the clutter.
3. Integrate the graphics and text.
4. Avoid the spaghetti chart.
5. Start with gray.
:::
:::
::::

# Visualizing Your Data

## Our Sample Dataset

::: {.fragment}
**Synthetic Donor Dataset for Fundraising Analytics** [@donor_data]

- Developed as a capstone project by Danielle Brown, M.S. Computer Science, Loyola Marymount University
:::

. . .

<u>Why this dataset?</u>

::: {.fragment}
- Not real donors
  - No real PII being shared
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Robust and well-rounded
  - 50,000 donor records
  - 484,954 giving history records
:::

::: {.incremental}
- One of very few advancement-specific open-source synthetic datasets
- Good for practicing machine learning (ML) models with advancement data
:::

## Types of Visualizations



# Case Studies

## Medication Effectiveness

These two charts are based on the same data...

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
![](./oxycontin_linear_redacted.jpeg){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
::: {style="text-align: right;"}
![](./oxycontin_logarithmic_redacted.jpeg){fig-align="center" width=90%}

*[...but they tell two different stories.]{.fragment}*
:::
:::
::::

[**What do you notice?**]{.fragment .fade-in-then-out} **[Any guesses as to which medication this is?]{.fragment .fade-in .highlight-orange}**

## [Medication Effectiveness]{.fragment .strike .semi-fade-out fragment-index="1"} [Drug Addictiveness]{.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="1"}

::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="1"}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
![](./oxycontin_linear.jpeg){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
![](./oxycontin_logarithmic.jpeg){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
::::
:::

::: {.r-stack}
::: {style="text-align: center;"}
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-semi-out}
Can you guess which graph Purdue Pharma used in their prescription inserts from December 1995 until August 2010?
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {style="font-size: 120%;"}
***[Data matters, but how you represent it matters, too.]{.highlight-orange}***
:::
:::
:::
:::

## The Curious Conundrum of the Baltic Giantess[(?)]{.fragment}

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![Source: *Reddit* user misteregamer1, posted to [r/CrappyDesign](https://www.reddit.com/r/CrappyDesign/comments/eqhtos/this_graph_comparing_average_womens_height_around/)](./avg_fem_height.png){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
[Okay, what's, uh...]{.fragment} [*unpolished* about this?]{.fragment .fade-in}

::: {.fragment}
- Vertical axis doesn't start at 0
- Column shapes are distracting
- Overlap creates visual clutter
- Color doesn't signify anything
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 120%;"}
*[Let's talk about audience.]{.fragment}*
:::
:::
::::

# Parting Thoughts

## Toward Good Data Stewardship

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 120%;"}
**[<u>Transparency fosters trust</u>.]{.fragment .highlight-orange}**
:::

::: {.incremental}
- As data stewards, our prime directive should be to accurately and honestly represent our data.
- Perceptive audiences *will* poke holes in visualizations that don't add up.
- Where data comes from can feel like a black box for non-specialists---be someone that you'd want to put your faith in.
- It's better to have the full picture than a convenient snippet.
:::

## The Artificial Intelli-phant in the Room

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 120%;"}
**[<u>Can't AI do all this for me</u>?]{.fragment .highlight-orange}**
:::

. . .

My answer?

::: {style="text-align:center;"}
[AI can do a lot of things for you.]{.fragment .highlight-orange}*[..with varying degrees of success and/or quality.]{.fragment .highlight-orange}*
:::

::: {.incremental}
- It's 2026. AI is everywhere---so yes, you absolutely could have AI create visuals for you.
  - AI can be a valuable tool when it comes to data analysis and coding visualizations.
- Validating AI output often requires familiarity *beyond* just the basics of data viz.
  - Every marathon runner first learned how to crawl.
- The quality of the input corresponds to the quality of the output.
  - Describing the details of your business problem and your request in a way that AI will understand is the foundation of prompt engineering.
:::

## Resources

## Let's Keep in Touch!
If you have any questions for me, feedback about my presentation, or requests for additional resources---including this slide deck---please reach out!

```{r qr-contact}
#| echo: false
#| include: false

contact_card <- qr_vcard(
  given = 'Kevin',
  family = 'Madden',
  email = c(work = 'kevin.madden@villanova.edu'),
  telephone = c(work = '610-519-7881'),
  organisation = 'Villanova University',
  job_title = 'Assistant Director of Annual Giving',
  photo = 'https://github.com/kfmadden/the-last-byte/blob/main/Kevin%20Madden_headshot.jpg?raw=true'
)

png(filename = 'KM_contact_card.png', bg = 'transparent')
par(mar = c(0, 0, 0, 0), oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0), xaxs = 'i', yaxs = 'i')
plot(contact_card, col = c('#282a36','#f8f8f2'), main = NA)
dev.off()
```

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width='40%'}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
![[kevin.madden@villanova.edu](mailto:kevin.madden@villanova.edu)](./KM_contact_card.png){width=250 height=250}
:::
:::

::: {.column width='60%'}
🙂 <u>Things I'm always happy to chat about</u> 👇

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width='40%'}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
📊 Data

💻 Coding

🧁 Baking
:::
:::

::: {.column width='40%'}
::: {style='text-align: center;'}
🎾 Tennis

📖 Book Recs

🐱 Your Pets
:::
:::
::::
:::
::::

## References
::: {refs}
:::
